messages.text: Venice lived by commerce, ships, credit, and reputation. Antonio was loved in that city because his money moved freely when friends were in need.
Yet he began this story sad without knowing why. His ships were at sea, but his heart felt heavier than any cargo.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: Antonio, I owe more debt than honor allows, but I know a way to restore both.

messages.name: Antonio
messages.text: Then speak. My purse and my person are open to you.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: In Belmont lives Portia, a lady of great fortune and greater virtue. Many suitors seek her, but I believe I may win her.

messages.name: Antonio
messages.text: My ready money is at sea. Still, use my credit and borrow what you need in Venice.
Bassanio went to Shylock, a Jewish moneylender who had long suffered insults from Antonio and answered them with cold patience.

messages.name: Shylock
messages.text: Three thousand ducats for three months. Antonio shall be bound. But should he fail, the bond will name a pound of flesh.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: That contract is monstrous. No friend should sign such risk for my courtship.

messages.name: Antonio
messages.text: My ships return before the date. The risk is a joke, and Shylock may learn mercy through kindness.

messages.name: Shylock
messages.text: Mercy? You spit on my coat, call me dog, and then ask for money. Let the bond be our proper friendship.
Antonio signed. Bassanio disliked the bond, but need and hope pressed him toward Belmont.
Far from Venice, Portia faced a different contract made by her dead father. She could not choose her husband freely.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: My fortune is large, Nerissa, but my choice is locked in three caskets. Gold, silver, and lead decide my life.

messages.name: Nerissa
messages.text: Your father was wise. The proper man will choose by character, not by show.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: Then let wisdom hurry. I am tired of princes who admire themselves before they greet me.
Thus two plots began: a bond in Venice and a choice in Belmont. One measured flesh, the other measured love.
Neither Antonio nor Portia yet knew how closely mercy and risk would bind their fortunes together.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: I will go with gratitude and fear. If I fail, the debt is more than money.
Antonio smiled as though friendship could make any contract harmless, and Venice believed him because his ships still rode the sea.
Antonio had lent money without interest and called that generosity. Shylock saw the same habit as an attack on his commerce and his dignity.

messages.name: Shylock
messages.text: You weaken my trade, then come to my door. Should I bend because your need has become polite?

messages.name: Antonio
messages.text: I am as likely to call you so again. Lend as to an enemy, if that suits you better.
The bitterness in that answer made the bond darker, but Antonio still trusted fortune. His confidence persuaded Bassanio against his fear.
In Belmont, Portia named each suitor with wit, yet the laughter could not unlock her choice. Her father's will ruled even after death.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: If doing nothing were proper obedience, I would be the most obedient woman in Italy.
Bassanio understood that friendship had become security for his desire. That knowledge did not stop him, but it made every borrowed ducat feel personal.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: If I win Portia, I must repay more than debt. I must prove Antonio right to trust me.
The bond therefore entered the story as both joke and threat. Antonio heard the joke because he trusted ships; Shylock kept the threat because he remembered insults.

messages.name: Shylock
messages.text: Call it merry if you like. I call it written.
